name: Windows Brute-Force Attack Detection and Response Playbook

action--03c2ab06-6cac-4e08-8e68-ff49d29b.name: Immediately investigate the alert to confirm potential compromise. Check the user account's recent activity and group memberships.

commands.command: Immediately investigate the alert to confirm potential compromise. Check the user account's recent activity and group memberships.

action--0a9c2d30-a152-4ea9-b8d2-f6fd9f48.name: Consider implementing account lockout policies to automatically slow down brute-force attacks, but be aware of potential denial-of-service implications.

commands.command: Consider implementing account lockout policies to automatically slow down brute-force attacks, but be aware of potential denial-of-service implications.
commands.description: Consider implementing account lockout policies to automatically slow down brute-force attacks, but be aware of potential denial-of-service implications.

action--1208dca4-81cb-492a-9d7a-005adf14.name: Incorporate logic to flag successful logons that occur from IP addresses not typically associated with the user or from geographic locations outside the norm.

commands.command: Incorporate logic to flag successful logons that occur from IP addresses not typically associated with the user or from geographic locations outside the norm.

action--1b6dc0ec-d8c2-4149-a402-2aa86695.name: If a compromise is suspected, immediately initiate password reset procedures for the affected user account.

commands.command: If a compromise is suspected, immediately initiate password reset procedures for the affected user account.

action--3462f29c-4093-40b6-978e-8f9b97d3.name: Temporarily block the source IP address of the attack at the network perimeter (firewall) or VPN concentrator to prevent further attempts.

commands.command: Temporarily block the source IP address of the attack at the network perimeter (firewall) or VPN concentrator to prevent further attempts.

action--3a0c04ef-1728-43bb-81c2-36ccd29c.name: The rule should trigger when the number of failed logons for a user:IP pair exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5-10 failures). This indicates a potential brute-force attempt.

commands.command: The rule should trigger when the number of failed logons for a user:IP pair exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5-10 failures). This indicates a potential brute-force attempt.

action--3ee04521-0b35-4c9b-8000-86746a32.name: Correlate this alert with any subsequent successful logon event (4624) for the same username from the same source IP address within a short time frame (e.g., 1 hour) after the failed attempt series.

commands.command: Correlate this alert with any subsequent successful logon event (4624) for the same username from the same source IP address within a short time frame (e.g., 1 hour) after the failed attempt series.

action--407ee9e3-834a-49c5-bdd2-207dc189.name: Flag successful logons that occur outside of normal business hours for that user or system if such baselines exist.

commands.command: Flag successful logons that occur outside of normal business hours for that user or system if such baselines exist.

action--6557a633-1a1b-44a4-84cf-d6b0050e.name: Configure log collection to ensure relevant events (Event IDs 4624, 4625) are being ingested into your SIEM or log analysis platform from all domain-joined Windows systems, especially critical servers and domain controllers.

commands.command: Configure log collection to ensure relevant events (Event IDs 4624, 4625) are being ingested into your SIEM or log analysis platform from all domain-joined Windows systems, especially critical servers and domain controllers.

commands.command: Establish a baseline of normal logon activity for your environment, including typical logon volumes, common source IPs, user accounts, and timeframes.
commands.description: Establish a baseline of normal logon activity for your environment, including typical logon volumes, common source IPs, user accounts, and timeframes.

action--7a9c5004-f573-4a32-be4b-1a093d73.name: If the account is confirmed compromised, follow incident response procedures for account takeover, including revoking active sessions and investigating for further lateral movement.

commands.command: If the account is confirmed compromised, follow incident response procedures for account takeover, including revoking active sessions and investigating for further lateral movement.

action--90abd5b0-db86-4c20-bd4f-ffc72892.name: Create a correlation rule that groups failed logon events (4625) by target username and source IP address within a defined time window (e.g., 5-15 minutes).

commands.command: Create a correlation rule that groups failed logon events (4625) by target username and source IP address within a defined time window (e.g., 5-15 minutes).

action--e617d35b-4f5e-435c-b4fa-cd6f1f67.name: Continuously review and adjust detection thresholds and time windows based on false positive rates and environmental changes.

action--fd438c20-d6d4-4a74-8b8a-0b543353.name: Identify the data sources required for detection: Windows Security Event Logs (Event ID 4625 for failed logons, Event ID 4624 for successful logons), and potentially network logs (e.g., firewall, VPN) for source IP context.

parallel--527bc57e-de66-4cd6-b052-47a29b.name: Implement immediate response actions upon alert generation.

parallel--d91ae169-eac6-4985-8b22-616d81.name: Develop and tune detection rules to identify the pattern of a high volume of failed logons followed by a successful logon.

description: This is an automatically generated playbook based on the question: A detections playbook for T1110 of MITRE ATT&CK Matrix for Enterprise framework is required.The target platform is Windows. You can find more information at https://attack.mitre.org/detectionstrategies/DET0463#AN1275 . The description of the approach follows: High volume of failed logon attempts followed by a successful one from a suspicious user, host, or timeframe

external_references.name: MITRE ATT&CK Technique T1110: Brute Force
MITRE ATT&CK Detection Strategy DET0463: High Volume of Failed Logon Attempts Followed by a Successful One

items.content: Identify the data sources required for detection: Windows Security Event Logs (Event ID 4625 for failed logons, Event ID 4624 for successful logons), and potentially network logs (e.g., firewall, VPN) for source IP context.

Configure log collection to ensure relevant events (Event IDs 4624, 4625) are being ingested into your SIEM or log analysis platform from all domain-joined Windows systems, especially critical servers and domain controllers.
Establish a baseline of normal logon activity for your environment, including typical logon volumes, common source IPs, user accounts, and timeframes.
Create a correlation rule that groups failed logon events (4625) by target username and source IP address within a defined time window (e.g., 5-15 minutes).
The rule should trigger when the number of failed logons for a user:IP pair exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5-10 failures). This indicates a potential brute-force attempt.
Correlate this alert with any subsequent successful logon event (4624) for the same username from the same source IP address within a short time frame (e.g., 1 hour) after the failed attempt series.
Incorporate logic to flag successful logons that occur from IP addresses not typically associated with the user or from geographic locations outside the norm.
Flag successful logons that occur outside of normal business hours for that user or system if such baselines exist.
Develop and tune detection rules to identify the pattern of a high volume of failed logons followed by a successful logon.
Immediately investigate the alert to confirm potential compromise. Check the user account's recent activity and group memberships.
If a compromise is suspected, immediately initiate password reset procedures for the affected user account.
Temporarily block the source IP address of the attack at the network perimeter (firewall) or VPN concentrator to prevent further attempts.
If the account is confirmed compromised, follow incident response procedures for account takeover, including revoking active sessions and investigating for further lateral movement.
Implement immediate response actions upon alert generation.
Continuously review and adjust detection thresholds and time windows based on false positive rates and environmental changes.
Consider implementing account lockout policies to automatically slow down brute-force attacks, but be aware of potential denial-of-service implications.
